title: Information is Physical Principle
definition: Information is not abstract or immaterial but is always encoded in physical states of systems. Every bit of information corresponds to a physical configuration, and information processing requires physical resources including energy, space, and time.
formula: E ≥ kT ln(2) per bit erased (Landauer's principle)

proofs.description: Landauer's principle establishes the physicality of information through energy dissipation in irreversible computing
proofs.reference: Landauer, R. (1961). Irreversibility and Heat Generation in the Computing Process. IBM Journal of Research and Development, 5(3), 183-191.

proofs.description: Experimental verification of Landauer's principle using colloidal particles and optical traps
proofs.reference: Bérut, A., et al. (2012). Experimental verification of Landauer's principle. Nature, 483, 187-189.

proofs.description: Maxwell's demon thought experiment demonstrates that information acquisition has thermodynamic consequences
proofs.reference: Bennett, C.H. (1982). The thermodynamics of computation—a review. International Journal of Theoretical Physics, 21, 905-940.

difficulty_levels.beginner: Information has to be stored somewhere physical - you can't have information without something physical to hold it. Even computer data exists as electrical charges or magnetic patterns.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: Information is tied to physical states; erasing information generates heat and is a physical process, not abstract. This connects computation directly to thermodynamics.
difficulty_levels.expert: The physicality of information bridges thermodynamics and computation, establishing fundamental limits on computing and connecting Shannon entropy to thermodynamic entropy. Landauer's principle provides the quantitative link: erasing one bit of information dissipates at least kT ln(2) energy.

extra_fields.historical_context: The principle emerged from Landauer's 1961 insight connecting computation to thermodynamics, resolving Maxwell's demon paradox. Bennett (1982) extended the work, showing that only logically irreversible operations (like erasure) require energy dissipation.
extra_fields.limitations: Applies to macroscopic systems; quantum information processing may have different constraints. The kT ln(2) bound is a minimum - real systems dissipate more.
extra_fields.philosophical_implication: Information cannot be separated from its physical substrate - mind and matter are entangled
